segments.s143-g89: نشست is a past third-person singular verb 'he sat' (here 'he sat down' or 'took his seat'). آنگهی is an adverb 'then/thereupon' placed after the verb in poetic order. رای زد is a compound where رای (opinion/counsel) is the noun and زد (lit. 'struck') functions as a light verb meaning 'to give/express' — together 'to deliberate/express a decision' or 'held counsel'. با + دبیر is a prepositional phrase 'with the secretary' marking the person he consulted; the subject ('he') is omitted but carried by the verbs.
segments.s143-g90: که introduces a subordinate (content) clause 'that …'. کاری functions as the noun 'an action/deed' (here used generically; none of the provided senses exactly match, so its sense is marked -1). گزاینده is a participial adjective derived from گزیدن ('to choose') and here modifies کاری, giving the sense of a decisive/choosing or consequential action. بد is an adjective 'bad/evil' modifying کاری, and ناگزیر is a predicative adjective 'inevitable/compelled' asserting that the action could not be avoided; the whole clause reports the content of the council's decision.
segments.s143-g49: بگفتش is the past 3rd‑person verb 'he said/told' with the enclitic -ش marking the indirect object 'to him/her'. چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the imperative that follows. رو here is an imperative 'go' (classical Persian imperative of رفتن), introduced by چنان; که begins a purpose/result clause 'so that' and is followed by the time adverbial فردا پگاه 'tomorrow at dawn'.
segments.s143-g50: نبیند is the negative present/subjunctive form (3rd singular) of دیدن used in the purpose clause introduced by که: 'may not see' or 'does not see' in the sense of 'so that ... not see'. ت is the 2nd‑person enclitic object ('you') attached to the verb phrase and را is the definite object marker. هیچکس is the indefinite 'no one', زان is 'from/of that' qualifying سپاه 'army/host'. Together: 'that no one of that army will see you.'
segments.s143-g51: فرستاد is the past 3rd‑person singular 'he sent/dispatched'. نامه is the direct object 'the letter'. سوی راه راست is a prepositional phrase 'toward the straight/direct road' (سوی = toward, راه = road/way, راست = 'straight/direct' modifying راه). Word order is verb → object → direction (typical Persian).
segments.s143-g52: پس and آنگاه are adverbials both meaning 'then/afterwards' (stylistic doubling common in verse). نامه here is again the subject; بر پای خاست is a verbal phrase meaning 'rose/stood up on foot' or idiomatically 'set out on foot' (بر = on/upon, پای = foot, خاست = past 'rose/got up'). The clause says the letter (poetically animated) immediately set out.
segments.s143-g53: بنه is 'bundle/pack' (the traveler's pack). برنهاد is an older past form 'he put/placed on' (بر + نهاد = 'set upon' or 'shouldered'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. سراندر is a fused locative 'سر اندر' meaning 'head inside' or 'with his head within/covered', and کشید here is the past of کشیدن meaning 'drew/pulled' (i.e. 'drew his head in/covered his head'); the verb is used transitively with سراندر as its object/locative complement.
segments.s143-g54: بران is 'on that/thereupon' linking to the preceding action. راه = 'road/way'. بی‌راه literally 'without the road' (here poetically 'astray/off the road'); شد is the past auxiliary 'became' or 'became so' and is used to form 'became off‑road/astray'; ناپدید means 'vanished/disappeared'. The whole clause: 'thereupon he went off the road and vanished.'
segments.s143-g55: سوی شهر ایران نهادند روی is a full clause 'they set/placed their faces toward the city of Iran' (سوی = toward, شهر ایران = the city of Iran, نهادند = past 3rd‑plural 'they set/placed', روی = 'face' used idiomatically as 'they turned their faces' = 'they turned toward'). The verb is plural to agree with the subject 'they'.
segments.s143-g56: سپردند is past 3rd‑plural 'they entrusted/handed over'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. باره here functions like 'burden/charge' or 'the matter/responsibility' (i.e. what was to be left/entrusted). دژ بدوی is 'the old/original fortress' (دژ = fortress; بدوی = original/ancient). Together: 'they entrusted that charge / they handed over the old fortress.'
segments.s143-g17: چو begins a concessive comparison ('though/as'); آواز او is the noun + possessive pronoun 'his voice' (noun before pronoun as usual). رعد غرنده is a nominal phrase 'roaring thunder' with غرنده as an adjective modifying رعد, and نیست is the present negative copula 'is not' linking the predicate to the subject 'آواز او'.
segments.s143-g18: چو again marks the parallel concessive clause. بازوی او is 'his arm' (noun + possessive), تیغ برنده is a predicate nominal 'a cutting/keen blade' (تیغ 'sword/blade', برنده 'sharp/cutting' as adjective), and نیست negates the copula: 'is not'. The word order is standard Persian nominal predicate with the copula at the end.
segments.s143-g19: هجیر دلاور is a name with an epithet in apposition ('Hejir the valiant'). میان را is the definite/direct-object phrase marked by را meaning 'the middle/center', and ببست is a past tense verb 'he closed/blocked' — together: 'Hejir the valiant closed the middle.'
segments.s143-g20: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in one instant' or 'suddenly' (یکی = one, باره = instance/occasion). تیزتگ is a small/keen blade (an instrument noun), and برنشست is a past action meaning 'alighted on/rose upon' (literally 'sat upon' in older usage) — so: 'suddenly a sharp blade alighted.'
segments.s143-g21: بشد here is a past/episodic auxiliary meaning 'he came/it happened that he became' and with پیش gives 'he came before/in the presence of'. سهراب is the proper name, and رزم‌آزمای is an epithet 'battle-tested/warrior' describing him. Word order places the verb before the locative phrase in this archaic poetic line.
segments.s143-g22: بر اسپش is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon his steed' (بر = on, اسپش = his horse/steed). ندیدم is first-person past negative 'I did not see'; فزون functions as 'greater/more splendid'; زان = 'than that' (literally 'from that'); به پای = 'at the foot/hoop/footing' (literal 'at the foot'). The whole clause says the speaker has seen nothing more splendid than that on the horse's foot — a compressed, poetic comparative.
segments.s143-g23: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that/so that'). بر هم زند is a verbal phrase meaning 'to strike together' → 'to close' (here 'to close the eyelids'); هم is the 'together' element, زند is the verb root (archaic 3rd-singular present/optative in this poetic context). مژه را is the definite object 'the eyelid/eyelash' with را marking it, and جنگ‌جوی is the affected noun 'warrior' (the one who would be made to blink).
segments.s143-g24: گراید is a present-tense verb 'turns/leans/starts to move'; ز بینی 'from the nose' (ز = from, بینی = nose/sense of smell) indicates source; سوی = 'toward'; مغز = 'the brain/core'; بوی = 'the scent/smell'. The clause literally says 'the scent turns from the nose toward the brain.'
segments.s143-g57: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic). خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. بر زد سر is a compact poetic idiom: زد normally 'strike', but here functions idiomatically with سر to mean 'lifted/raised its head' or 'appeared/rose' (past tense, perfective). از تیره‌کوه 'from/out of the dark mountain' uses از + compound noun تیره‌کوه 'dark mountain' to mark origin/location.
segments.s143-g58: میان را is the definite object/topic 'the middle' (را marks it as definite/emphatic). ببستند is a third‑person plural past 'they shut/closed' (perfective). ترکان گروه 'bands of Turks' places the agent (ترکان) before the noun گروه 'group/troop', i.e. 'Turkish bands' — the agent of the verb ببستند.
segments.s143-g59: This is a left‑anchored noun phrase: سپهدار سهراب 'Sohrab the commander' (title + name), followed by نیزه بدست 'spear in (his) hand' — بدست here means 'in the hand' (instrumental/adverbial phrase describing posture).
segments.s143-g60: یکی بارکش bاره‌ای is a compact nominal clause: یکی 'one / a certain' + بارکش 'pack‑animal / porter' + باره‌ای 'laden with a load' (باره here = 'load'). برنشست is past tense 'sat upon / mounted' — the verb applies to the preceding nominal (he mounted a single laden pack‑animal).
segments.s143-g61: سوی باره آمد is 'came toward the pack/load' (سوی = toward(s), باره = the load/pack). یکی بنگرید 'one looked' — یکی here 'a person / someone' and بنگرید is an older/poetic 3rd‑person past or imperative‑style form meaning 'he looked' or 'he cast his eyes'. The clause describes approach + glance.
segments.s143-g62: به باره درون 'into/inside the pack' (به used as 'into/towards' + درون 'inside'). بس کسی را ندید is negative: بس here is 'merely/at all' emphasizing absence, کسی 'anyone' and را is the definite/object marker; ندید is past negative 'he did not see'. Together: 'he saw no one at all inside the pack.'
segments.s143-g63: بیامد 'he came' (past). در دژ 'to/in the fortress' (در = in/at). گشادند باز is a terse poetic clause: گشادند (lit. 'they opened' or 'they made wide' — 3rd plural past) and باز here qualifies it as 'open' (i.e. the gates were opened). Word order is compact: arrival followed by the action of opening.
segments.s143-g64: ندیدند در دژ 'they did not see in the fortress' — ندیدند is 3rd‑person plural past negative. یکی رزمساز 'a single/one warrior' follows as the (absent) object: یکی used as the numeral 'one' and رزمساز 'warrior'. The whole clause means 'they saw not one warrior in the fortress.'
segments.s143-g9: یکی here is an indefinite pronoun 'a certain / one' placed before the noun; پهلوانی is an archaic/poetic nominal form referring to a champion/hero (used like 'a champion'). به پیش اندرون is a locative phrase: به پیش = 'into the presence / before' and اندرون = 'the inner place / inside', so the whole segment means 'a certain champion within, before them.'
segments.s143-g10: که introduces a relative clause modifying the champion: 'whose ...'. سالش is 'his years / his age' (سال + enclitic ش = 'his age'). ده و دو is the archaic way to state twelve ('ten and two'). نباشد فزون literally 'is not more' — نباشد is the classical negative copular/optative used here in narration to mean 'is not / was not', and فزون 'greater/in excess'. The clause means 'whose age was twelve and not more.'
segments.s143-g11: به بالا is an adverbial phrase 'in height / aloft'; ز introduces the standard poetic comparative ('from' = 'than') so ز سرو سهی = 'than the dark cypress' (سهی is a poetic epithet meaning dark/black). برترست is the predicate 'is superior / is loftier.' The line uses a comparative construction: 'In height he is loftier than the dark cypress.'
segments.s143-g12: چو is a simile 'as / like'; خورشید تابان = 'the shining sun' (noun + epithet). به دو پیکرست is a poetic predicate: پیکرست is an adjectival/verbalized form 'is-bodied / has a body', so the phrase 'به دو پیکرست' means 'he is (as) two-bodied' or 'as if in two forms.' The construction compares him to the shining sun and then states this hyperbolic 'two-bodied' image.
segments.s143-g13: This is a compact, poetic description: برش here functions as a possessive/nominal phrase referring to 'his figure/appearance' (an older usage), followed by چون بر پیل 'like an elephant' (چون = 'like') and بالاش برز = 'and his height/stature is lofty' (بالا + ش = 'his height', برز = 'high/exalted'). There is no overt finite verb in the first clause; the predicates are adjectival/nominal and coordinated for vivid comparison.
segments.s143-g14: ندیدم is simple past negative 'I did not see'; followed by کسی را (indefinite object marked by را). چنان 'such' modifies the following coordinated noun phrase دست و گرز. Here دست is used in the extended sense 'arm / power / skill in handling weapons' (not merely 'hand'), and گرز is 'mace/club'. The clause means 'I have seen no one with such arm/skill and mace.'
segments.s143-g15: چو is temporal 'when / as'; شمشیر هندی is 'an Indian sword' (noun + adjectival epithet). به چنگ آیدش is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'comes into his grasp/hand' — به چنگ آمدن = 'to come into (someone's) hand'. آیدش is third-person present/narrative 'it comes to him' with enclitic ش marking 'to him'.
segments.s143-g16: This line lists two similes of origin with parallel prepositions: ز دریا 'from the sea' and از کوه تنگ 'from the narrow mountain/pass' (ز and از are both poetic 'from'). آیدش repeats the verb 'it comes (to him)'. The repetition and the dual sources create the image that the sword's movement/impact seems to come from both sea and mountain.
segments.s143-g73: چو here is the simile/introduction 'as/like' used repeatedly to enumerate persons (an epic listing device). The sequence is a coordintion of names: طوس (Tūs, the hero) و (and) چو (again 'as/like') گودرز then another name; کشواد and گیو are appended with و. The verb is omitted (elliptical listing), so these nouns function as a linked apposition of people present.
segments.s143-g74: This continues the catalogue with another leading چو 'as/like' followed by names linked by و. گرگین, بهرام and فرهاد are proper names; نیو is the final noun in the series (here printed as a noun following the names). Again the line is nominal and elliptical — no finite verb in this segment.
segments.s143-g75: سپهدار is a title/person 'the commander'. نامه‌بر is the messenger/letter‑carrier (object of the action). بخواند here is used in the sense 'to call/summon' (an older/poetic use of خواندن); word order is Subject (سپهدار) + Object (نامه‌بر ایشان) + Verb (بخواند), so: 'the commander summoned the messenger to them.'
segments.s143-g76: بپرسید is a past/historical narration form 'he asked' (third person). بسیار here modifies پرسید and means 'many/much' ('he asked many questions' or 'he questioned him thoroughly'). و connects the next clause; خیره بماند is a compound where خیره 'astonished' describes his state and بماند 'he remained/was left' is the verb, so together 'and he remained astonished.'
segments.s143-g77: چنین 'thus/so' introduces reported speech or a manner of speaking. گفت here is the narrative 'said' (the lexeme is listed as a conversation/utterance noun in the glosses but functions as the past verb 'said'). با + پهلوانان marks the addressees 'to/with the champions'; براز follows as a modifier/epithet attached to پهلوانان in the manuscript sense (the lexical glosses give agricultural meanings, but syntactically it is grouped with 'pahlavânân' as their qualifier).
segments.s143-g78: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. این کار گردَد is a typical impersonal/future-looking expression where گردد (subjunctive/plainive of گشتن) = 'will/happen/become'; به ما بر دراز is a small prepositional phrase: به ما 'on/for us' and بر دراز 'for a long time/upon us long' (بر = on/upon, دراز = long). Together: 'that this matter will be long upon us.'
segments.s143-g79: برین سان is a fused poetic form meaning بر این سان 'in this way/like this' (بر 'on', ین an orthographic variant of این 'this', سان 'manner'). که introduces reported speech or attribution 'that/as'. گژدهم is a proper name; گوید همی is the habitual/present narrative 'he says' with همی giving the continual/iterative sense (poetic use of همی to mark ongoing/repeated action).
segments.s143-g80: از اندیشه دل را is an object phrase: از اندیشه 'from/of thought' modifying what happens to دل را (the heart, with را marking it as definite object). بشوید is a verbal form here meaning 'washes/cleanses' in context (the supplied glosses list auxiliary senses, but in classical usage شوییدن/شستن sense is intended); همی again marks the repeated/continuous aspect. Together: 'he continually washes the heart of thought' (i.e. frees the heart from thought).
segments.s143-g65: 'به فرمان' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by (the) order' (beh = 'by'), expressing manner/cause. 'همه' is the subject 'all/everyone' and 'پیش او' is an adverbial phrase 'before him / in his presence'. 'آمدند' is past tense, third-person plural 'they came'. Word order is somewhat inverted/poetic: manner + subject + place + verb.
segments.s143-g66: The phrase 'به جان هرکسی' is an emphatic/adverbial expression 'for the life of each (one)' (beh = 'for/on behalf of', جان = 'life'), adding urgency or an oath-like flavor. 'هرکسی' here means 'each person / everyone', 'چاره‌جو' is a compound noun/adjective 'seeker of a remedy', and 'آمدند' (they came) is past plural; together: 'each one came seeking a solution.'
segments.s143-g67: 'چو' here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. 'نامه' is the subject 'the letter', 'به نزدیک خسرو' is a prepositional/circumstantial phrase 'to/near Khosrow', and 'رسید' is the past tense 'reached/arrived'. Typical Old/poetic word order places the verb at the end of the clause.
segments.s143-g68: The verbless-looking clause is poetic inversion: 'غمی شد دلش' = 'his heart became sorrowful' (غمی = sorrow, شد = became, دلش = his heart). 'کان' is an archaic conjunctive/relative element introducing the reason clause ('that' / 'for'), and 'سخنها شنید' = 'he heard the words' (سخنها = the words/talks, شنید = heard). Together: 'his heart was grieved, for he heard those words.'
segments.s143-g69: Structure: definite/direct object 'گرانمایگان را' (the nobles/illustrious men + object marker را) + source phrase 'ز لشکر' ('from the army') + verb 'بخواند'. In epic diction 'بخواند' functions as a past/preterite form meaning 'he summoned/called (them)'. 'را' marks the definite object.
segments.s143-g70: 'وزین داستان' is a descriptive phrase 'and/of that weighty (solemn, important) tale' (وزین = dignified/weighty, داستان = tale). 'چندگونه' is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'in several/various ways' and 'براند' (poetic past form of راندن with prefix ب-) is a third-person past form meaning literally 'he drove/put forth' but here used figuratively as 'he set forth/related' the tale in different manners. Word order places the manner/adverbial before the verb.
segments.s143-g71: 'نشستند' is the past plural 'they sat/met/assembled' (poetic placement of the verb first). 'با شاه ایران' is the prepositional phrase 'with the king of Iran'. 'به هم' is an adverbial idiom 'together' (here reinforcing the collective sitting). The clause thus means 'they sat together with the king of Iran.'
segments.s143-g72: 'بزرگان لشکر' is a genitive/noun phrase 'the great ones/chiefs of the army'. 'همه' quantifies them 'all', and the idiom 'بیش و کم' literally 'more and less' means 'greater and lesser' or 'more or less' — i.e., everyone, high and low. The phrase lists who sat: the army's chiefs, in their entirety.
segments.s143-g33: بران is a terse, poetic locative ('on that' / 'thereon') placed at the start; کوه بخشایش is a genitive-like compound 'a mountain of forgiveness' (کوه + بخشایش). آرد is 3rd-person singular classical Persian 'brings' / 'brought' (verb) and is placed before its subject زمین (poetic inversion). زمین is the subject 'the earth/ground'.
segments.s143-g34: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he'; اسپ is 'horse/steed'; تازد is the 3rd-person singular form of تاخت meaning 'gallops / rushes' (here habitual or characteristic action). برو روز کین functions as a prepositional temporal phrase 'on the day of vengeance' (برو here is an archaic/poetic form corresponding to بر + روز).
segments.s143-g35: عنان‌دار is a compound adjective 'reins-bearing' (one skilled with the reins). چون introduces a simile 'like/as'; او is 'he'. ندیدست is the negative perfect/past-participle-form meaning 'has not been seen' (i.e., 'no one has seen'); کس here occurs in the scope of the negative and yields the sense 'no one'.
segments.s143-g36: تو گفتی is reported speech ('you said') — گفتی is the 2nd-person past in classical diction; که introduces reported content 'that'; سام is the proper name; سوارست is a predicative compound 'is mounted / is a horseman'; و بس is a short concluding phrase 'and that is enough' (بس = enough / merely).
segments.s143-g37: بلندیش is 'his/its loftiness/height' (بلندی + ضمیر); بر آسمان رفته is a participial clause 'has gone up to the sky/heaven' (رفته = gone); گیر here is an imperative 'take/seize!' addressed to an agent (poetic, abrupt command following the description).
segments.s143-g38: سر بخت is a genitival phrase 'the head of fortune' (سر = head/top, بخت = fate/fortune). گردان here is an imperative-like verbal form 'turn/bring about' (in this context commanding the turning of fortune's head). همه خفته is 'all (those) asleep' (همه = all; خفته = asleep/laid low), and the final گیر repeats the imperative 'seize/capture'.
segments.s143-g39: اگر is the conditional 'if'; خود emphasizes the subject 'we ourselves'; شکیبیم is 1st-person plural present/subjunctive 'we are patient / we can be patient' (classical form); یک چند is an adverbial phrase 'for a short while / a little while' (چند = a few); نیز 'also/too' here reinforces the small duration ('for a little while even').
segments.s143-g40: نکوشیم is the hortatory/subjunctive 1st-person plural negative 'let us not strive / let us not attempt' (ن + کوشیم). و is the conjunction 'and'; دیگر here means 'anymore / any further'; نگوییم is the negative subjunctive 'let us not say' (let us say no more); چیز is the general noun 'thing/matter' used in the formula 'نگوییم چیز' = 'say nothing'.
segments.s143-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برگشت is a past (3rd sg.) verb 'returned' placed before the subject (a common Classical Persian order: verb then subject). سهراب is the subject (proper name). گژدهم پیر is a noun phrase apposed/connected to the following object in the next segment: 'Gozhdaham the elder' (pیر is an epithet 'old/elder').
segments.s143-g2: بیاورد is past tense 'brought' (3rd sg.). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بنشاند is an archaic/poetic past of نشاندن, a causative meaning 'set/seat (someone)' (hence the second sense). مردی is an indefinite noun 'a man' and دبیر is an appositive identifying him as 'a scribe/scholar' (order: noun then apposition).
segments.s143-g3: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (here introducing someone). نامه بنوشت intends 'wrote a letter' — بنوشت is an archaic past form of نوشتن (but the provided sense list does not match this verbal use, so the sense index is set to -1). نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase 'near the king' (نزدیک governed the verb 'placed/cast' in the next segment).
segments.s143-g4: برافگند is a past of برافکندن used here as 'sent/cast forth' (literally 'threw out/dispatched'). پوینده is a participle/adjective 'moving, going' (i.e. a messenger or one who goes) modifying مردی 'a man' (indefinite). به راه marks direction 'on/along the road'. The sequence shows a single agent dispatching a man toward the road.
segments.s143-g5: نخست is an adverb 'first'. آفرین here means 'praise/words of praise' (an acclamation) and is combined with کرد (past of کردن) to form 'gave praise' or 'praised'—a common Classical Persian collocation. بر کردگار is the prepositional phrase 'to/upon the Creator' (کردگار = God).
segments.s143-g6: نمود is an older past form of نمودن meaning 'appeared/showed' (here 'was revealed/appeared'). آنگهی is an adverb 'then'. گردش روزگار is a noun phrase 'the turning/cycle of fate/time' (گردش = 'turn, rotation' is the head, modified by روزگار 'fate/time').
segments.s143-g7: که is a conjunction/relative 'that' introducing the clause. آمد is past 'came'. بر ما is the prepositional phrase 'upon/against us' (بر used in the sense 'against'). سپاهی گران is the noun + adjective 'a heavy/weighty (i.e. formidable) army' (adjective follows noun in Persian).
segments.s143-g8: همه is 'all/everyone' modifying the following plural. رزم جویان is a compound meaning 'battle-seekers' (i.e. warriors eager for combat); جویان is a plural participial element 'seekers'. کندآوران is a plural agentive/participle-form word, here functioning as an additional descriptor/appositive; the provided sense list does not fit this poetic usage, so its sense index is set to -1.
segments.s143-g25: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('that' / 'who'). سهرابش is the proper name سهراب with the enclitic -ش marking the object ('him' — i.e. 'Sohrab [seized] him'). از پشت زین is a prepositional phrase 'from behind the saddle' (از = from; پشت = back/behind; زین = saddle). برگرفت is a past tense verb 'took up/ seized' and completes the clause.
segments.s143-g26: برش here denotes a physical 'cut' or gash (noun) left by the action; ماند is the past 'remained/was left'. زان (= 'from that' / 'therefrom') links this result back to the previous clause. بازو means 'arm' and اندر شگفت is an archaic locution 'in/with wonder' (اندر = in; شگفت = wonder), describing that the state of the arm caused astonishment.
segments.s143-g27: درست‌ست is a copular phrase 'it is right/true'. و اکنون simply links and times the next comment ('and now'). به زنهار اوست uses به with زنهار ('caution/警告') and the copula اوست to say 'he is in a state of caution' — i.e. 'he is on his guard.'
segments.s143-g28: پراندیشه is an adjectival/nominal phrase 'full of thought / anxious'; جان ('soul, life') is its patient/possessive focus. از پی کار means 'after / following his business'—از پی = 'in pursuit of' and کار = 'affair/deed/task'. اوست is the copula 'it/he is', so the clause says his soul is anxious and intent on his affairs.
segments.s143-g29: سواران ترکان is a noun phrase 'riders of the Turks' (noun + genitive). بسی is an adverb modifying دیده‌ام ('many' / 'often' / 'a lot'), and دیده‌ام is the perfect first-person 'I have seen.' Word order places the object phrase before the verb, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s143-g30: عنان پیچ is a nominal compound 'twisting of the reins' (عنان = reins; پیچ = twist/turn). زین‌گونه is an adverb 'in this manner/so'. نشنیده‌ام is the negative perfect 'I have not heard (of)', here meaning 'I have never heard/seen of such a thing.'
segments.s143-g31: مبادا که is a conventional negative-purpose/exhortation 'lest / may it not be that'. او is 'he'. در میان دو صف is the locative phrase 'in the midst of / between the two ranks' (در = in; میان = between; دو صف = two ranks/lines). The clause sets up a feared possibility.
segments.s143-g32: یکی مرد جنگ‌آور is 'one man, a warrior' (یکی = a certain/one; مرد = man/hero; جنگ‌آور = warrior). آرد is the classical-present/subjunctive form of آوردن 'bring' used here to express potentiality ('should bring' / 'should seize'), and بکف lit. 'into the hand' = 'into his grasp' (archaic phrase). Together they mean 'should seize/bring a single warrior into his hand/grasp.'
segments.s143-g81: This line contains two coordinated questions: 'چه سازیم' (= 'what shall we do?') where 'سازیم' is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive form (classical/poetic usage giving a hortatory/rhetorical question). After the conjunction 'و' the second clause 'درمان این کار چیست' is a normal nominal question 'what is the remedy of this affair' with the copula 'چیست' (چی + ست) at the end.
segments.s143-g82: This clause asks about the identity of a man described by preceding material. Word order is somewhat inverted: 'از ایران هم آورد' (literally 'also came/was brought from Iran') precedes 'این مرد' and ends with the interrogative-copula 'کیست' ('who is'). 'آورد' is simple past 3rd-person used narratively ('came/was brought'), and 'هم' marks 'also/even'.
segments.s143-g83: 'بر آن برنهادند' is a fixed classical/periphrastic phrasing meaning 'they resolved/decided upon that'; 'برنهادند' is past 3rd‑person plural. 'یکسر' is an adverb 'entirely/at once' modifying the action, and the particle 'که' introduces the subordinate clause (the content of their decision): 'that Giv...'.
segments.s143-g84: This is the subordinate clause introduced by 'که': 'به زابل شود' = 'should go/become at Zabul' where 'شود' is subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person (classical use meaning 'may go / should go'). 'نزد سالار نیو' is a location/presence phrase 'to/into the presence of the commander Niyu' with 'نزد' meaning 'in the presence of / at the court of'.
segments.s143-g85: 'به رستم رساند از این آگهی' is a reportative clause: 'he/they conveyed to Rostam this news.' 'رساند' is past 3rd‑person (narrative 'delivered/reported') and the prepositional phrase 'به رستم' marks the recipient; 'از این آگهی' specifies the content/source as 'about this news'.
segments.s143-g86: The clause after 'که' reports the content: 'با بیم شد تخت شاهنشهی' — literally 'the throne of the shahanshah became with fear.' Here 'شد' is past 'became' and 'با بیم' ('with fear/ alarm') adverbially qualifies the change; 'تخت شاهنشهی' is the noun phrase 'the imperial throne' (threatened/ shaken). This is a poetic way to say the imperial rule is endangered.
segments.s143-g87: 'گو' is a quotative/imperative introductory particle here ('say/command'), followed by the object 'پیلتن را' (Piltan with the definite/object marker 'را'). 'بدین رزمگاه' = 'to this battlefield' (classical contraction of 'به + این'), where 'ب' is the prepositional element 'to/into' and the final noun 'رزمگاه' is 'battlefield/place of battle.' The whole phrase functions as an imperative command: 'Tell/summon Piltan to this battlefield.'
segments.s143-g88: 'بخواند' here is the verb 'to call/summon' (subjunctive/imperative sense after the quotative), and 'که' introduces the explanatory clause. 'اویست' is presented as a proper name/identifier (here taken as 'Owis'); 'پشت سپاه' is a genitival phrase 'the rear of the army' with 'پشت' = 'rear/behind' and 'سپاه' = 'army.' The clause gives the reason/role: 'for Owis is the army's rear.'
segments.s143-g41: چو 'اگر' introduces a conditional clause ('if'). 'دم زند' is a poetic expression: 'دم' (literally 'breath/moment') is the object and 'زند' is the verb meaning 'utter/strike/breathe' (here used idiomatically = 'make a move'). 'شهریار زمین' is a noun phrase 'the sovereign of the land' (noun + noun; 'زمین' specifies the realm). Word order is typical classical Persian: condition marker + object+verb + the ruler phrase.
segments.s143-g42: Two negative verbs appear: 'نراند' and 'نسازد' are classical/epic-present forms with the preverbal negative 'ن-' (here: 'does not/will not drive away' and 'does not/will not make'). 'نراند سپاه' = 'drive away the army' and 'نسازد کمین' = 'make an ambush'; the coordinating 'و' links the two verb phrases.
segments.s143-g43: 'دژ و باره گیرد' = 'he seizes the fortress and the baggage/booty': 'گیرد' is a present/simple (he takes/seizes). 'باره' here means load/booty. 'که' begins an explanatory clause 'for/because', and 'خود زور هست' literally 'he himself has strength' (خود = emphatic 'himself/own', هست = copula 'is').
segments.s143-g44: 'نگیرد' is a negative present form ('does not / will not seize'); in negative context 'کسی' functions as 'no one'. The clause 'دست او را به دست' is idiomatic/literal 'take his hand by the hand'—here meaning 'lay hands on him' or 'seize him'; 'را' marks the definite object 'his hand'.
segments.s143-g45: The particle 'که' introduces an explanation/reason. 'این باره را' = 'this occasion/turn' with 'را' marking it as definite object/topic. 'نیست پایاب اوی' literally 'there is no outlet/escape for him': 'نیست' negates existence, 'پایاب' here has the old sense 'outlet/escape', and 'اوی' is the classical genitive/possessive 'his'.
segments.s143-g46: 'درنگی شود' is a noun + auxiliary construction meaning 'a pause/delay occurs' ('درنگی' = a pause; 'شود' = happen/become). 'شیر زاشتاب اوی' = 'the lion of his haste' (زاشتاب = 'from/with haste' fused form; 'اوی' = 'his'), an image meaning that if he delays the (hasty) lion will act—verb completes in following lines.
segments.s143-g47: 'چو' is a temporal conjunction 'when'. 'نامه به مهر' = 'the letter with a seal' ('به' here indicating instrument/association and 'مهر' = 'seal'), 'اندر آمد' = 'came in/arrived' and 'به شب' specifies time 'at night' (classical order places temporal phrase after the verb).
segments.s143-g48: 'فرستاده را جست' = 'he sought/looked for the messenger'—'را' marks the definite object and 'جست' here means 'sought' (not literal 'leapt'). 'و بگشاد لب' = 'and opened (his) lip'—'بگشاد' is the verb 'opened' (action sense) and 'لب' 'lip'; opening one's lip is an idiom for beginning to speak.

sentences.s143-s45: He sat down, then held counsel with the secretary, deciding that a grievous action was unavoidable.
sentences.s143-s25: He told him, "Go in such a way that at dawn tomorrow no one of that army will see you."
sentences.s143-s26: He sent the letter toward the straight road; then the letter at once set out on foot.
sentences.s143-s27: He shouldered the bundle and drew his head within (or pulled up his cloak); on that road he went astray and vanished.
sentences.s143-s28: They turned their faces toward the city of Iran; they handed over that charge and the old fortress.
sentences.s143-s9: Though his voice is not a roaring thunder, his arm is not a cutting sword.
sentences.s143-s10: Hejir the valiant closed off the middle; suddenly a sharp blade alighted.
sentences.s143-s11: He came before Sohrab, battle-tested; on his steed I have seen nothing finer than that at the hoof.
sentences.s143-s12: that would make the warrior close his eyelids; the scent turns from the nose toward the brain.
sentences.s143-s29: When the sun lifted its head above the dark mountain, bands of Turks shut off the middle (pass).
sentences.s143-s30: Sohrab the commander, spear in hand, mounted a laden pack‑animal.
sentences.s143-s31: He came up to the pack and looked in; he saw that there was no one inside the load.
sentences.s143-s32: He came to the fortress; they opened the gates, but they saw not a single warrior inside the citadel.
sentences.s143-s5: There was a certain champion inside before them, whose age was twelve and no more.
sentences.s143-s6: Loftier than a dark cypress, he is like the shining sun—as if he had two bodies.
sentences.s143-s7: His form is like an elephant and his stature is lofty as a mountain; I have seen no one with such hand and mace.
sentences.s143-s8: When an Indian sword comes into his hand, it seems to come from the sea and from the narrow mountain.
sentences.s143-s37: There were Tous and Goudarz, Kashvâd and Giv; Gorgin, Bahram and Farhad, and the ney.
sentences.s143-s38: The sepahdar summoned the messenger to them; he asked many questions and stood astonished.
sentences.s143-s39: He said thus to the champions (pahlavânân) — that this affair will lie upon us for a long time.
sentences.s143-s40: In this fashion — as Gozdeham says — he keeps saying: out of thought he continually washes the heart.
sentences.s143-s33: By the order, all came before him; each one came, begging for a remedy on his life.
sentences.s143-s34: When the letter reached near Khosrow, his heart became sorrowful, for he heard those words.
sentences.s143-s35: He summoned the nobles from the army, and with that weighty tale he related it in various ways.
sentences.s143-s36: They sat together with the king of Iran — the great men of the army, all of them, greater and lesser.
sentences.s143-s17: The earth brought forth a mountain of forgiveness thereon for him who spurs his steed on the day of vengeance.
sentences.s143-s18: No one has seen anyone reins-bearing like him; you said that Sam is a rider, and that is enough.
sentences.s143-s19: His loftiness has gone up to the heavens — seize it; turn the head of fortune and take all the sleeping.
sentences.s143-s20: If we ourselves can be patient for a little while, let us not strive and say nothing further.
sentences.s143-s1: When Sohrab returned, he brought Gozhdaham the elder and seated a scribe.
sentences.s143-s2: A certain man wrote a letter, placed it near the king, and dispatched a man along the road.
sentences.s143-s3: First he gave praise to the Creator; then the turn of fate was revealed.
sentences.s143-s4: For a heavy (formidable) army came upon us — all eager for battle and combatants.
sentences.s143-s13: Sohrab seized him from behind the saddle; a gash remained on that arm, to everyone's astonishment.
sentences.s143-s14: It is right, and now he is on his guard; his soul is full of care and intent on his task.
sentences.s143-s15: I have seen many Turk riders, but I have never heard of reins being twisted like this.
sentences.s143-s16: Lest, between the two ranks, he should seize a single warrior in his grasp.
sentences.s143-s41: What shall we do, and what is the remedy for this matter? Who is this man who has come from Iran?
sentences.s143-s42: They at once resolved on that—that Giv should go to Zabul to the commander Niyu.
sentences.s143-s43: He conveyed this news to Rostam: that, amid alarm, the imperial throne had been shaken.
sentences.s143-s44: He said, 'Summon Piltan to this battlefield,' for Owis is the rear of the army.
sentences.s143-s21: If the sovereign of the land stirs (utters a breath), he will not drive the army away nor make an ambush.
sentences.s143-s22: He seizes the fortress and the booty; for he himself has strength, no one can take his hand.
sentences.s143-s23: For this turn there is no outlet for him; if he delays, the lion, in his haste, (will ...).
sentences.s143-s24: When the sealed letter arrived at night, he sought the messenger and opened his lips.
